Part 1 

1. What are the variables used in the data set and describe them?

The variables used are a mix between continuous and categorical. The ozone measurement used in the data set is a continuous, dependent variable, while the gardens that are measured in the study for ozone content are categorical, independent variables. 

2. What is the type of statistical test needed to compare the first data set?

The type of test needed here will be a one sample t-test. 

3. What is the null hypothesis?

There is no relationship between the gardens location and the ozone levels contained in the gardens. 

4. What is the alternative hypothesis?

There is a relationship between the location of the gardens and the ozone levels of the gardens. 

```{r}
#reading the first data set

ozone <- read.csv("data/Dataset1_ozone.csv")
str(ozone)

#check for normality using a histogram

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(ozone, aes(x = Ozone)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "skyblue", color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of Ozone Levels", x = "Ozone Levels", y = "Occured")

#run the one sample t-test

t.test(ozone$Ozone, mu = 50, alternative = "greater")

#add summary stats

summary(ozone$Ozone)

```
